summary: This advice column addresses three substantive questions: whether a parent should spend savings on travel versus hoarding for children's college, how to cope with dating exhaustion in the age of Tinder, and a clarification about a crepe recipe. The dating advice is the centerpiece, offering a sociological critique of how dating apps have commoditized romance and made the 'dating apocalypse' visible. O'Neil argues that many men have learned to repress their feelings to 'win' at dating, treating it like any other competitive system. She advises the lovelorn questioner to seek out idea-focused social spaces and approach new connections as potential friendships rather than romantic prospects.
key_points: Dating apps like Tinder haven't created the commodification of love—they've just made it more visible and universal
Many men excel at learning arbitrary rules of power systems and exploiting them ruthlessly, whether in academics, finance, or dating
The solution to dating exhaustion is to invest in friendships rather than romantic pursuits, as 'friend bucks' may eventually become convertible currency
Parents shouldn't make themselves miserable for the sake of college savings, especially when creative solutions like subletting exist
Seeking out idea-focused spaces like meetups and Nerd Nites is more likely to yield genuine connections than dating apps
money_quotes: We've entirely commoditized sex, love, and even affection, and especially in places like New York where there are so many beautiful and single women, the single man feels like an idiot for settling with one.
I think (many) men are better at learning the rules of a system and exploiting them viciously to their benefit.
I've always been amazed how quickly the men around me adapt to the petty and arbitrary rules of power and status, whether in academics, finance, or engineering startups.
It is by no means obvious that we should make ourselves miserable for the sake of college costs.
You might have only $10 to spend on love, but you might have thousands of friend bucks in the bank.
Tinder is making every place feel like New York.
key_insight: The modern dating market operates like any other competitive system where some players learn to repress their humanity to 'win,' making genuine connection increasingly difficult to find through conventional channels.
